text: The surgeon has completed the mobilization of the small and large bowel mesentery and is now skeletonizing the bowel to prepare for anastomosis, ensuring clear separation of the bowel from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon aligns the terminal ileum and colon, indicating a need to extend the mesenteric dissection more proximally than initially performed.
The surgeon advances along the mesentery of the small bowel, staying close to the bowel, towards the next pedicle artery of the mesentery.
The surgeon demonstrates the mobilization and control of the mesentery, ensuring it is adequately freed for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon successfully navigates underneath the small bowel, but encounters difficulty accessing the area beneath the colon due to the mesentery.
The surgeon ensures the mesentery is fully skeletonized off the bowel to prevent its incorporation into the staple line, which could lead to bleeding due to the non-hemostatic nature of the staplers.
The surgeon mobilizes underneath the colon, carefully dissecting and skeletonizing the mesentery along the colon to ensure it is separated from the bowel.
The surgeon demonstrates how the mesentery falls away smoothly, achieving a perpendicular orientation to the bowel, facilitating clear visualization and manipulation.
The surgeon demonstrates the skeletonization of the bowel on both sides, allowing the stapler to be smoothly positioned across the bowel to fire the common staple line and complete the anastomosis.
